segments.inferno-16-g1: Già means “by then,” placing the scene at a point already reached in the journey; era has an implied first-person subject, “I was.” Onde is a relative adverb meaning “from which/where,” and s’udia is an impersonal or passive construction, “one heard/was heard”; cadea is the archaic imperfect for cadeva, “was falling.” Ne l’altro giro means “in/into the other circle,” with poetic contraction of in + il.
segments.inferno-16-g2: Simile a links this sound to a comparison: “similar to” or “like.” Quel che means “that which/the sound which,” and l’arnie is plural, “the beehives”; fanno rombo is a cognate-style expression meaning “make a rumbling/humming sound.”
segments.inferno-16-g3: Quando introduces the main event that happens while Dante is in that place. Tre ombre is the subject, and si partiro is an archaic form of si partirono, “they separated themselves/broke away,” with si used reflexively.
segments.inferno-16-g4: Correndo is a gerund, “running,” explaining the manner in which the three shades broke away. It attaches to the action of si partiro rather than forming a separate finite clause.
segments.inferno-16-g5: D’una torma depends on si partiro: they broke away “from a troop.” Che passava is an imperfect relative clause, “that was passing,” and sotto la pioggia de l’aspro martiro describes the punitive rain under which the troop moves; de l’ is the poetic form of dell’.
segments.inferno-16-g6: Venian is the poetic shortened form of venivano, “they were coming.” Ver’ is an apocopated form of verso, “toward,” so ver’ noi means “toward us.”
segments.inferno-16-g7: E coordinates this clause with the preceding movement toward Dante and Virgil. Ciascuna is feminine because it refers back to ombre, and gridava is imperfect, describing repeated or ongoing crying out as they approached.
segments.inferno-16-g8: Sòstati is an imperative with an enclitic reflexive pronoun, literally “stop yourself,” addressed emphatically with tu. Ch’ means “because,” and a l’abito means “by/from your clothing or appearance”; ne is an old form meaning “to us” here. Sembri esser alcun di nostra terra prava means “you seem to be someone from our wicked town,” with esser as the dependent infinitive after sembri.
segments.inferno-16-g9: Ahimè is an exclamation of grief, “alas.” It introduces Dante’s emotional reaction before the description of what he saw.
segments.inferno-16-g10: Che is exclamatory here, meaning “what,” not simply “that.” Vidi is the first-person past definite, “I saw,” and ne’ is the contraction of nei, “in/on the”; lor is the archaic possessive “their.”
segments.inferno-16-g11: Ricenti e vecchie are feminine plural adjectives agreeing with piaghe, “wounds.” The pairing contrasts fresh wounds with older scars, emphasizing continuing punishment.
segments.inferno-16-g12: Da le is the older separated form of dalle, “by the.” Incese is a feminine plural past participle agreeing with piaghe: the wounds are “burned” or “set aflame” by the falling flames.
segments.inferno-16-g13: Ancor means “still,” and men duol is not “hurts less” here: men represents me ne, so the phrase means “it grieves/pains me about it.” Pur ch’i’ introduces the idea “even/merely that I,” with i’ an elided form of io; me ne rimembri is a pronominal construction meaning “I remember it/recall it,” with rimembri as a subjunctive-like old form after che.
segments.inferno-16-g87: “Però” is an inferential connector, looking back to what has just been said and introducing the request that follows: “therefore” or “so.”
segments.inferno-16-g88: The clause is conditional: “se” means “if,” followed by present subjunctive forms “campi” and “torni,” referring to a possible future event. “D’esti” is an archaic contraction of “di esti,” meaning “from these,” and “torni a riveder” uses “tornare a” plus infinitive: “return to see again.”
segments.inferno-16-g89: “Quando” introduces the future time at which the condition will matter: “when.” “Ti gioverà” is future of “giovare,” literally “it will be pleasing/useful to you,” with “ti” as an indirect object; “dicere” is an archaic infinitive for “dire,” and “I’ fui” is “Io fui,” “I was.”
segments.inferno-16-g90: “Fa che” is an imperative construction meaning “make sure that” or “see that,” followed by the subjunctive “favelle.” “Di noi” means “of/about us,” and “a la gente” is the older separated form of modern “alla gente,” “to the people.”
segments.inferno-16-g91: “Indi” is a narrative adverb meaning “then.” “Rupper” is a shortened preterite form of “ruppero,” “they broke,” and “la rota” means the circular formation or wheel in which the spirits had been moving.
segments.inferno-16-g92: “A fuggirsi” is a poetic infinitive phrase, roughly “in fleeing away” or “as they fled.” The word order is inverted for poetry: the subject is “le gambe loro isnelle” (“their swift legs”), and “ali sembiar” means “seemed wings.”
segments.inferno-16-g93: “Un amen non saria possuto dirsi” is a passive conditional construction: literally, “an amen would not have been able to be said.” “Saria” is an archaic form of “sarebbe,” “possuto” is the past participle of “potere,” and “e’ fuoro” is archaic for “they were”; the comparison means they disappeared faster than one could say “amen.”
segments.inferno-16-g94: “Per ch’” is “per che,” meaning “wherefore” or “for which reason,” linking their sudden disappearance to the next action. “Al maestro parve di partirsi” means “it seemed fitting to the master to depart,” with “parve” used impersonally and “partirsi” as a reflexive/poetic infinitive for “to depart.”
segments.inferno-16-g95: “Io lo seguiva” uses the imperfect tense for ongoing narrative action: “I was following him.” “Seguiva” is an archaic or poetic first-person singular form where modern Italian would normally say “seguivo.”
segments.inferno-16-g96: “Poco eravam iti” literally means “we were gone little,” that is, “we had gone only a little way.” “Eravam” is an apocopated form of “eravamo,” and “iti” is an old past participle meaning “gone.”
segments.inferno-16-g97: Here “che” introduces the result or circumstance after “we had gone only a little way”: the sound had become very near. “De l’acqua” is the separated form of modern “dell’acqua,” and “n’era” uses “ne” in an old pronominal sense referring to “to us,” though that sense is not represented in the listed options.
segments.inferno-16-g98: The second “che” introduces the consequence of the water’s loud nearness. “Per parlar” is an infinitive phrase meaning “in order to speak” or “for speaking,” and “saremmo ... uditi” is conditional passive, “we would be heard”; “a pena” is the old separated spelling of “appena,” “scarcely.”
segments.inferno-16-g52: S’i’ is a poetic contraction of se io, “if I”; fossi stato ... coperto is a past counterfactual subjunctive passive construction, “had been covered/sheltered.” Dal foco is best understood as “from the fire,” with foco an archaic form of fuoco.
segments.inferno-16-g53: Gittato mi sarei is an inverted conditional-perfect reflexive construction: normal prose order would be mi sarei gittato, “I would have thrown/cast myself.” Tra lor means “among them,” and di sotto functions adverbially as “down below.”
segments.inferno-16-g54: Credo che introduces a subordinate clause, here with the conditional avria sofferto, an archaic form of avrebbe sofferto. ’l dottor is “the teacher/master,” referring to Virgil, and l’ before avria is not the article but the object pronoun lo, “it”: “would have allowed it.”
segments.inferno-16-g55: Ma perch’ means “but because,” giving the reason Dante did not act on the wish just described. Mi sarei brusciato e cotto is reflexive conditional perfect: “I would have burned and scorched myself,” with two participles intensifying the physical danger.
segments.inferno-16-g56: The word order is poetic: vinse paura la mia buona voglia means “fear overcame my good desire,” with paura as subject and voglia as object. The relative che refers to voglia; di loro abbracciar is an infinitive phrase, “to embrace them,” and facea is archaic faceva, “made,” in “made me eager.”
segments.inferno-16-g57: Poi marks sequence, “then.” Cominciai is first-person singular passato remoto, “I began,” introducing Dante’s direct speech.
segments.inferno-16-g58: Non dispetto is an elliptical fronted object: “not contempt.” The noun dispetto here means disdain or scorn, set up for contrast with the following ma doglia.
segments.inferno-16-g59: Ma doglia supplies the contrasting subject/object idea: “but grief.” The main verb fisse is an archaic past-tense form from figgere, “fixed/fastened”; the poetic order means “your condition fixed grief within me.”
segments.inferno-16-g60: Tanta che forms a result clause: “so great that.” Si dispoglia is reflexive, literally “strips itself off,” meaning that the grief will only slowly be shed or pass away; tutta reinforces completeness, “wholly.”
segments.inferno-16-g61: Tosto che means “as soon as,” linking the onset of Dante’s grief to Virgil’s earlier words. Questo mio segnor is “this lord/master of mine,” and per le quali means “through/by means of which”; i’ mi pensai is “I thought to myself.” The correlative qual ... tal begins here: “such as you are...”
segments.inferno-16-g62: Tal completes the correlative qual ... tal: “people such as you.” Venisse is imperfect subjunctive in indirect thought after mi pensai che, “I thought that ... was coming.”
segments.inferno-16-g63: Di vostra terra sono places the prepositional phrase first for emphasis: literally, “of/from your land I am.” Terra here means city or homeland, specifically Florence, not merely earth or soil.
segments.inferno-16-g64: Sempre mai is an emphatic poetic expression meaning “always, ever.” L’ovra di voi and li onorati nomi are the objects of ritrassi e ascoltai; ovra is a poetic form of opera, “work/deeds,” and li is an older masculine plural article, modern i/gli.
segments.inferno-16-g65: Lascio lo fele contrasts bitterness with hope: fele means “gall,” figuratively bitter suffering. Vo is an archaic/poetic form of vado, “I go,” and vo per dolci pomi means “I go in search of sweet fruits.” Promessi a me per lo verace duca modifies pomi: the fruits have been promised to Dante by the true guide.
segments.inferno-16-g66: ’Nfino is a contracted poetic form of infino, “as far as/up to,” and al centro means “to the center,” namely the earth’s center at the bottom of Hell. Pria convien ch’i’ tomi means “first it is necessary that I fall/descend”; ch’i’ contracts che io, and tomi is a subjunctive form governed by convien.
segments.inferno-16-g67: Se here means “if,” introducing a courteous conditional wish, and conduca is present subjunctive: literally, “if the soul may long lead your limbs.” The elevated phrasing treats the soul as the thing that guides the living body, meaning “if you live long.”
segments.inferno-16-g68: Rispuose is an archaic form of rispose, “answered,” in the preterite. Quelli means “that one,” referring to the speaker among the three, and ancora means “again,” since he continues or resumes speaking.
segments.inferno-16-g69: This second se is again conditional, parallel to the first: “and if your fame shines after you.” Luca is a poetic present subjunctive form from lucere, “to shine,” matching conduca and expressing a wish for Dante’s posthumous renown.
segments.inferno-16-g70: The imperative dì, “tell,” governs the indirect question se dimora, “whether there still dwells.” Ne la is the older separated spelling of nella, “in the,” and sì come suole means “just as it is accustomed to do,” though suole is an archaic/poetic form of “is wont,” not the noun “sole.”
segments.inferno-16-g71: This clause offers the alternative to the previous indirect question: “or whether it has gone wholly outside.” Del tutto is an idiom meaning “completely,” and se n’è gita fora combines reflexive/idiomatic se with ne, “from there/away,” plus è gita, an old feminine past participle agreeing with the implied pair cortesia e valor collectively or with the idea of virtue.
segments.inferno-16-g72: Ché introduces the reason for their concern: “for/because Guglielmo Borsiere...” The name is a historical proper name; Guiglielmo is Dante’s form for Guglielmo, “William.”
segments.inferno-16-g73: Il qual is a relative pronoun, “who,” referring back to Guglielmo Borsiere. Si duole means “grieves” or “laments,” with si as part of the reflexive verb dolersi; per poco means “for a little while,” indicating he has only recently been with them, and va là coi compagni places him among the other damned companions.
segments.inferno-16-g74: Assai intensifies the verb: “greatly.” Ne here functions as an old object pronoun meaning “us,” though that sense is not in the supplied list; con le sue parole indicates the means by which he distresses them, “with/by his words.”
segments.inferno-16-g75: La gente nuova and i sùbiti guadagni are the compound subject, while orgoglio e dismisura are the objects produced. Han is the shortened form of hanno, and generata is the past participle of generare; the feminine singular ending is poetic and influenced by the nearer object dismisura.
segments.inferno-16-g76: Fiorenza is a direct vocative address to Florence. Dante interrupts the syntax to name the city being accused.
segments.inferno-16-g77: In te completes the idea begun earlier: these vices have been generated “in you,” namely in Florence. The pronoun te refers to the personified city addressed in the vocative.
segments.inferno-16-g78: Sì che means “so that,” introducing the result of the moral corruption just named. Ten is a contraction of te ne, “you for it/of it,” and piagni is an archaic form of piangi, “you weep.”
segments.inferno-16-g79: Così refers back to the words just spoken: “thus.” Gridai is first-person singular preterite, “I cried out,” and con la faccia levata describes Dante’s posture, “with his face raised.”
segments.inferno-16-g80: E links Dante’s cry to the reaction of the three souls. I is the definite article in i tre, “the three,” though the numeral itself is not included in this segment’s word list.
segments.inferno-16-g81: Che is a relative pronoun referring to “the three.” Inteser is an apocopated/poetic preterite form of intesero, “they understood,” and per risposta means “as an answer,” with per marking function or role.
segments.inferno-16-g82: Guardar is a shortened poetic form of guardarono, “they looked.” L’un l’altro means “one another,” and com’ al ver si guata means “as one gazes at the truth,” with si used impersonally; guata is a verb meaning “looks/gazes,” not represented by the supplied senses.
segments.inferno-16-g83: Se means “if,” opening a conditional clause. L’altre volte means “the other times/on other occasions,” and sì poco ti costa means “it costs you so little,” with ti as the dative pronoun, “to you.”
segments.inferno-16-g84: Rispuoser is a shortened poetic preterite form of rispuosero/risposero, “they answered.” Tutti makes clear that all three souls speak together.
segments.inferno-16-g85: Il satisfare is a nominalized infinitive, literally “the satisfying,” and it is the delayed subject of costa: “if satisfying others costs you so little.” Altrui means “others” or “other people.”
segments.inferno-16-g86: Felice te is an exclamatory construction: “fortunate you!” The second se again means “if,” sì means “thus/so,” and a tua posta is an idiom meaning “at your pleasure/as you wish,” not the ordinary noun “post” or “station.”
segments.inferno-16-g32: This opens a conditional clause: “Se” here means “if,” not the verb “you are.” The subject is “miseria d’esto loco sollo,” with archaic “esto” for “this” and “sollo” meaning loose or yielding; “rende in dispetto noi e nostri prieghi” places the objects after the verb, meaning that the place’s misery makes them and their prayers despised.
segments.inferno-16-g33: The reporting clause interrupts the quoted speech. “Cominciò l’uno” has normal past-tense narration: “one of them began,” with “l’uno” referring to one member of the group already present in the scene.
segments.inferno-16-g34: This phrase continues the conditional idea from the first segment: “and if our stained and stripped appearance” also makes them contemptible. “’l” is an elided form of “il,” and “brollo” is an archaic adjective meaning bare, stripped, or peeled-looking.
segments.inferno-16-g35: The main clause is optative/jussive: “la fama nostra ... pieghi” means “may our fame bend/persuade your mind.” “A dirne” depends on “pieghi,” but “ne” here functions archaically as “to us,” so the sense is “to tell us who you are”; “se’” is the contracted second-person form of “sei,” “you are.”
segments.inferno-16-g36: This relative clause describes “tu”: “you who rub/wear your living feet through Hell.” “I vivi piedi” stresses that Dante is alive, and “sicuro” is adverbial in force, meaning that he moves confidently or fearlessly.
segments.inferno-16-g37: “Questi” is a demonstrative pronoun meaning “this one” or “this man.” It introduces the first of the companions whom the speaker is identifying.
segments.inferno-16-g38: This is a relative insertion modifying “Questi.” “L’orme di cui” means “whose tracks,” and “pestar mi vedi” has the infinitive “pestar” governed by “vedi”: literally, “you see me tread.”
segments.inferno-16-g39: “Tutto che” is an archaic concessive expression meaning “although.” The subjunctive “vada” is used because the clause is concessive: “although he goes/is going naked and stripped,” with “dipelato” meaning hairless or flayed-looking.
segments.inferno-16-g40: The main verb “fu” is a simple past, “he was.” “Di grado maggior” means “of greater rank,” while “che tu non credi” is a comparative clause in which “non” is expletive and does not make the meaning negative in English: “than you think.”
segments.inferno-16-g41: This identifies his lineage: “nepote fu” keeps the predicate before the verb for poetic emphasis. “De la” is the older separated form of modern “della,” and “nepote” here means grandson or descendant.
segments.inferno-16-g42: “Guido Guerra ebbe nome” is an idiom meaning “he had the name Guido Guerra,” i.e. “he was named Guido Guerra.” “Guerra” is a surname here, not the common noun “war.”
segments.inferno-16-g43: “In sua vita” means “during his life.” “Fece ... assai” is elliptical: he “did much,” and the two instrumental phrases “col senno” and “con la spada” mean by wisdom/counsel and by military action.
segments.inferno-16-g44: “L’altro” means “the other one,” introducing the second companion. The article plus adjective functions substantively, as a noun phrase.
segments.inferno-16-g45: The relative “ch’” refers back to “l’altro.” “Appresso me” means near or beside me, and “la rena trita” has a poetic word order: the object “la rena” precedes the verb “trita,” meaning that he treads or wears down the sand.
segments.inferno-16-g46: This is the main identification: “is Tegghiaio Aldobrandi.” The verb “è” links “l’altro” with the proper name.
segments.inferno-16-g47: “La cui voce” means “whose voice,” but the sense is his counsel or public opinion. “Nel mondo sù” means “in the world above,” the living world, and “dovria esser gradita” is an archaic conditional passive phrase: “should be welcomed/esteemed.”
segments.inferno-16-g48: “E io” introduces the speaker himself after he has named the other two. The pronoun is emphatic: “and I.”
segments.inferno-16-g49: This relative clause modifies “io.” “Posto son” is an inverted passive construction, equivalent to “son posto,” “I am placed”; “in croce” is figurative here, meaning in torment or affliction rather than literally on a cross.
segments.inferno-16-g50: “Iacopo Rusticucci fui” places the name before the verb for emphasis and meter. “Fui” is first-person singular past, “I was,” used because he is speaking as a dead soul about his earthly identity.
segments.inferno-16-g51: “Certo” is adverbial, “certainly.” “Più ch’altro” means “more than anything else,” and “mi nuoce” takes “mi” as the person harmed: “harms me”; “fiera” is adjectival here, meaning fierce or savage.
segments.inferno-16-g14: The prepositional phrase “A le lor grida” means “at/to their cries” and is placed first for emphasis. “Il mio dottor” is Dante’s guide-teacher, and “s’attese” is a past-tense reflexive/medio-passive form meaning that he attended or gave heed to the cries.
segments.inferno-16-g15: “Volse” is the simple past of “volgere,” here “turned,” with “’l viso” as its object. “Ver’ me” is an apocopated poetic form of “verso me,” meaning “toward me.”
segments.inferno-16-g16: “E” links the action of turning with the direct speech that follows. “Or” means “now,” and “aspetta” is a second-person singular imperative: Virgil tells Dante to wait.
segments.inferno-16-g17: “Disse” is the simple past of “dire,” introducing Virgil’s spoken words. The verb is placed after the quoted command, a normal reporting construction.
segments.inferno-16-g18: “A costor” means “to these people/these men,” with “costor” referring to the approaching souls. “Si vuole esser cortese” uses impersonal “si” plus “vuole” in the sense “it is required/one must,” followed by the infinitive phrase “esser cortese.”
segments.inferno-16-g19: “Se non fosse” is an imperfect subjunctive condition: “if it were not.” In “il foco che saetta la natura del loco,” the relative “che” refers to the fire as the thing shot, while “la natura del loco” is the postposed subject: literally, “the fire which the nature of the place shoots like arrows.”
segments.inferno-16-g20: “I’” is the poetic shortened form of “io,” “I,” though the supplied lemma does not reflect that. “Dicerei” is an archaic/poetic conditional, “I would say,” and “stesse” is an imperfect subjunctive of “stare” meaning “would befit”; “che ... che” forms the comparison “better to you than to them.”
segments.inferno-16-g21: “Ricominciar” is an apocopated third-person plural past tense, for “ricominciarono,” meaning “they resumed/began again.” Its subject appears later as “ei,” “they.”
segments.inferno-16-g22: “Come” here has a temporal force, “as/when.” “Noi restammo” means “we stopped/stood still,” with “restammo” from “restare,” not in the sense of amazement supplied in the list.
segments.inferno-16-g23: “Ei” is the delayed subject, “they,” referring to the three souls. “L’antico verso” is the object of “Ricominciar”: literally “the old verse,” meaning their former cry, refrain, or accustomed utterance.
segments.inferno-16-g24: “Quando” introduces the next temporal clause, “when.” “Fuor giunti” is poetic for “furono giunti,” “had come/reached,” with the auxiliary form contracted; “a noi” means “to us.”
segments.inferno-16-g25: “Fenno” is an old form of “fecero,” “they made.” “Una rota di sé” means that they formed a wheel or circling ring out of themselves, and “tutti e trei” is the archaic form of “all three.”
segments.inferno-16-g26: “Qual” is used correlatively with the later “così”: “as ... so.” “Sogliono ... far” means “are accustomed to do,” and “nudi e unti” modifies “i campion,” evoking wrestlers or fighters who are naked and oiled.
segments.inferno-16-g27: “Avvisando” is a gerund giving simultaneous action: they are watching for, estimating, or judging their chance. “Lor presa e lor vantaggio” means “their hold and their advantage,” with “lor” functioning as a possessive.
segments.inferno-16-g28: “Prima che” introduces a temporal clause requiring the subjunctive, hence “sien,” “they may be/are.” “Tra lor” has a reciprocal sense, “among/by one another,” and “punti” here is from “pungere,” meaning “pricked, stabbed, wounded,” not the noun “point.”
segments.inferno-16-g29: “Così” completes the comparison begun by “Qual”: “so/thus.” “Rotando” is a gerund meaning “wheeling/turning around,” describing the manner in which the three souls move.
segments.inferno-16-g30: “Ciascuno” is singular, so the verb “drizzava” is also singular: “each one was directing.” “Il visaggio” is the object, “the face,” and “a me” marks the direction, “toward me.”
segments.inferno-16-g31: “Sì che” forms a result clause, “so that.” “’N contraro” is poetic for “in contraro,” “in the opposite direction,” and “faceva ... continüo vïaggio” personifies the neck as continually traveling counter to the feet while the body circles.
segments.inferno-16-g116: This is an impersonal construction: “convien che” means “it is necessary that,” followed by the subjunctive “risponda.” “Novità” is the subject of “risponda,” and “pur” adds emphatic force, roughly “surely” or “indeed.”
segments.inferno-16-g117: “Dicea” is an archaic imperfect form for “diceva,” here meaning “I was saying.” “Fra me medesmo” is literally “among/between myself,” an idiom meaning “to myself” or “within myself.”
segments.inferno-16-g118: “Al novo cenno” completes “risponda”: the strange thing must respond “to the new/strange sign.” The relative clause “che ’l maestro con l’occhio sì seconda” describes that sign; “seconda” is from “secondare,” meaning that the master follows or supports it with his gaze, though the provided senses do not list that verbal meaning.
segments.inferno-16-g119: “Quanto cauti” is an exclamatory phrase: “how cautious.” “Esser dienno” means “must be,” with “dienno” used poetically for “devono/debbono”; “presso a color che” means “near/in the presence of those who.” “Non veggion pur l’ovra” means they do not see merely the outward work or act.
segments.inferno-16-g120: “Ma” contrasts the previous clause: they see not merely the deed, but something deeper. “Per entro i pensier” is a poetic phrase meaning “through/inside the thoughts,” while “miran col senno” means they look or discern by means of understanding.
segments.inferno-16-g121: “El” is an archaic form of “egli,” meaning “he.” “Disse a me” uses the preposition “a” with the indirect object: “he said to me.”
segments.inferno-16-g122: “Tosto verrà” is future: “soon will come.” “Di sovra” means “from above,” and “ciò” is the subject of “verrà”; the two relative clauses, “ch’io attendo” and “che il tuo pensier sogna,” identify it as the thing Virgil awaits and Dante’s mind imagines.
segments.inferno-16-g123: “Convien ch’...” again means “it is necessary that,” taking the subjunctive “si scovra.” “Al tuo viso” means “to your sight,” and the reflexive “si” makes “scovra” mean “reveal itself.”
segments.inferno-16-g124: The sentence begins with a general maxim: “l’uom” means “a person/one,” not a specific man. “De’” is a contracted poetic form of “deve,” “must/should,” governing the infinitive “chiuder”; “a quel ver c’ha faccia di menzogna” means “against/to that truth which has the appearance of a lie.” “Fin ch’el puote” means “as long as he can,” with “el” as an old form for “he.”
segments.inferno-16-g125: “Però che” functions as a causal conjunction, “because.” The phrase “sanza colpa” means “without fault/guilt,” and “fa vergogna” means “causes shame” or “brings disgrace.”
segments.inferno-16-g126: “Ma” introduces Dante’s exception to the rule he has just stated. “Tacer nol posso” contains “nol” = “non lo,” literally “I cannot keep it silent,” meaning “I cannot be silent about this.”
segments.inferno-16-g127: “Per le note” is an oath formula: Dante swears “by the notes/verses” of the poem. “Di questa comedìa” identifies the work itself, with “comedìa” referring to Dante’s own poem.
segments.inferno-16-g128: “Lettor” is a direct vocative address to the reader. It interrupts the oath to make the appeal more solemn and personal.
segments.inferno-16-g129: “Ti” is the indirect object, “to you.” “Giuro” is present tense: “I swear,” continuing the oath begun with “per le note.”
segments.inferno-16-g130: “S’elle” means “se elle,” “if they,” referring back to the “note” or verses of the poem. “Sien” is subjunctive, and the clause is an oath-condition: “may they not be devoid of lasting grace.”
segments.inferno-16-g131: “Ch’i’ vidi” means “that I saw,” completing what Dante swears. The construction is “vidi ... venir notando una figura”: literally “I saw a figure come swimming,” with “notando” used in the old sense “swimming.” “Per quell’aere grosso e scuro” means “through that thick and dark air,” and “in suso” means “upward.”
segments.inferno-16-g132: “Maravigliosa” agrees with “figura” and describes the figure as astonishing. “Ad ogne cor sicuro” means “to every secure/steadfast heart,” implying that even the bravest would marvel at it.
segments.inferno-16-g133: “Sì come” introduces a simile: the rising figure is like a diver returning upward. “Colui che va giuso talora a solver l’àncora” means “one who sometimes goes down to loosen the anchor”; “ch’aggrappa” is a relative clause describing the anchor that has hooked onto a rock or something else hidden in the sea.
segments.inferno-16-g134: This final relative clause still describes the diver in the simile. “’N sù” means “upward”; “si stende” is reflexive, “stretches himself,” while “da piè si rattrappa” means he draws himself together from or at the feet as he rises.
segments.inferno-16-g99: Come begins a long comparison whose matching così comes later: “as ... so ...”. c’ha is poetically compressed for che ha, “which has”; prima means “first,” identifying the river as the first to take its own eastward course from Monte Viso.
segments.inferno-16-g100: This prepositional phrase continues the geographical description of the river’s course. costa here is a noun meaning “slope/side,” not the verb “costs”; da la is the older uncontracted form of modern dalla.
segments.inferno-16-g101: che refers back to the river and introduces a relative clause. si chiama is the reflexive/passive idiom “is called,” and suso means “up above,” i.e. upstream or in its upper course.
segments.inferno-16-g102: avante che means “before,” taking the subjunctive divalli because it introduces a subordinate temporal clause. si divalli is a reflexive/intransitive poetic form, “descends down,” and nel basso letto means “into the lower riverbed.”
segments.inferno-16-g103: This clause adds that, once it reaches Forlì, the river no longer bears the name Acquacheta. è vacante di quel nome literally means “is vacant/devoid of that name,” a Latinate construction.
segments.inferno-16-g104: rimbomba is the main verb of the comparison’s first half: the river “roars/reverberates.” per cadere expresses cause or manner, “by falling,” and dovea ... esser recetto means “was meant/ought to be a shelter,” with esser as the infinitive dependent on dovea.
segments.inferno-16-g105: così completes the correlative comparison begun by Come: “as that river roars, so we found this water resounding.” It marks the return from the geographical simile to Dante’s immediate scene.
segments.inferno-16-g106: This phrase gives the place from which the sound came: “down from a steep bank.” d’una is contracted di una, and discoscesa is a poetic adjective meaning steep or precipitous.
segments.inferno-16-g107: trovammo is first-person plural past definite, “we found.” risonar is an infinitive depending on trovammo, as in “we found the water resounding”; quell’acqua tinta means the dark, stained, or colored water of the infernal stream.
segments.inferno-16-g108: sì che means “so that,” introducing the result of the tremendous noise. avria is a poetic conditional form of avrebbe, “would have,” and l’orecchia offesa literally means “would have hurt the ear.”
segments.inferno-16-g109: avea is an archaic/imperfect form of aveva, “I had.” cinta is a past participle used adjectivally with corda, meaning the cord was “girt” or fastened around him; intorno supplies “around.”
segments.inferno-16-g110: con essa refers back to the cord. pensai prender uses pensare in the sense “intend/plan,” and alcuna volta means “at one time” or “once”; a la pelle dipinta is a descriptive phrase, “with the painted/spotted skin.”
segments.inferno-16-g111: Poscia ch’ means “after that/when,” introducing a completed prior action. l’ refers to the cord, and ebbi ... sciolta is a compound past form: “I had loosened/untied it”; tutta emphasizes the whole cord or the completeness of the action.
segments.inferno-16-g112: sì come means “just as.” m’avea comandato is pluperfect in sense, “had commanded me,” with m’ as indirect object, “to me.”
segments.inferno-16-g113: porsila is a compressed form of porsi la, “I handed/presented it,” with la referring to the cord. a lui is the indirect object, and aggroppata e ravvolta describe the cord as knotted and coiled when given.
segments.inferno-16-g114: Ond’ here means “therefore/whereupon,” linking Virgil’s action to Dante handing him the cord. ei is “he,” si volse is reflexive “turned himself/turned,” and inver’ lo destro lato means “toward the right side,” with lo as an older article form.
segments.inferno-16-g115: alquanto di lunge da la sponda means “somewhat far from the edge,” with da la again an uncontracted form. la before gittò is a pronoun referring to the cord, while quell’alto burrato uses alto in the sense “deep,” since the ravine extends far downward.

sentences.inferno-16-s1: By then I was in a place from which one could hear the rumble of the water that was falling into the next circle, like the rumbling sound that beehives make, when three shades together broke away, running, from a troop that was passing beneath the rain of the harsh torment.
sentences.inferno-16-s2: They were coming toward us, and each one was crying: “Stop, you, for by your dress you seem to us to be someone from our wicked city.”
sentences.inferno-16-s3: Alas, what wounds I saw on their limbs, fresh and old, burned by the flames!
sentences.inferno-16-s4: It still pains me, even merely to remember it.
sentences.inferno-16-s21: Therefore, if you escape from these dark places and return to see the beautiful stars again, when it will please you to say, “I was there,” see that you speak of us to the people.
sentences.inferno-16-s22: Then they broke the circle, and as they fled their swift legs seemed wings.
sentences.inferno-16-s23: An amen could not have been said as quickly as they vanished; therefore it seemed good to my master to depart.
sentences.inferno-16-s24: I followed him, and we had gone only a little way when the sound of the water was so near us that, to speak, we would scarcely have been heard.
sentences.inferno-16-s13: If I had been sheltered from the fire, I would have thrown myself down among them, and I believe that the Teacher would have allowed it; but because I would have burned and scorched myself, fear overcame my good desire, which made me eager to embrace them.
sentences.inferno-16-s14: Then I began: “Not contempt, but grief did your condition fix within me, so great that it will be slow to strip itself wholly away, as soon as this lord of mine told me words through which I thought that people such as you are were coming.
sentences.inferno-16-s15: I am from your city, and always, ever, with affection I recounted and heard of your deeds and your honored names.
sentences.inferno-16-s16: I leave the gall and go in search of the sweet fruits promised to me by the truthful guide; but first it is necessary that I descend as far as the center.”
sentences.inferno-16-s17: “If your soul may long guide your limbs,” that one answered again, “and if your fame shines after you, tell us whether courtesy and valor dwell in our city as they used to, or whether they have gone wholly out of it; for Guglielmo Borsiere, who has been grieving with us for only a short while and goes there with his companions, greatly distresses us with his words.”
sentences.inferno-16-s18: “The new people and the sudden gains have bred arrogance and excess in you, Florence, so that you already weep for it.”
sentences.inferno-16-s19: Thus I cried with my face raised; and the three, who understood that as an answer, looked at one another as one looks at truth.
sentences.inferno-16-s20: “If on other occasions it costs you so little,” they all answered, “to satisfy others, happy are you if you speak thus at your pleasure!”
sentences.inferno-16-s9: And one began: “If the misery of this loose ground makes us and our prayers contemptible, and if our stained and stripped appearance does too, may our fame bend your mind to tell us who you are, you who with living feet pass so confidently through Hell.”
sentences.inferno-16-s10: This man, whose tracks you see me treading, although he goes naked and stripped of hair, was of higher rank than you think: he was the grandson of the good Gualdrada; Guido Guerra was his name, and in his life he did much with wisdom and with the sword.
sentences.inferno-16-s11: The other, who beside me treads the sand, is Tegghiaio Aldobrandi, whose voice should have been welcomed in the world above.
sentences.inferno-16-s12: And I, who am placed with them in torment, was Iacopo Rusticucci; and certainly my fierce wife harms me more than anything else.”
sentences.inferno-16-s5: At their cries my teacher gave heed; he turned his face toward me, and, “Now wait,” he said, “one must be courteous to these men.”
sentences.inferno-16-s6: And if it were not for the fire that the nature of the place shoots down, I would say that haste would be more fitting for you than for them.”
sentences.inferno-16-s7: They resumed, as we stopped, their old refrain; and when they had reached us, all three made a wheel of themselves.
sentences.inferno-16-s8: As naked, oiled champions are accustomed to do, watching for their hold and their advantage before they are struck and wounded by one another, so, wheeling around, each kept directing his face toward me, so that his neck made a continuous journey in the opposite direction from his feet.
sentences.inferno-16-s29: “Surely some strange thing must answer,” I was saying to myself, “to the strange sign which the master so follows with his eye.”
sentences.inferno-16-s30: Ah, how cautious men must be near those who see not only the outward deed, but look with their understanding into the thoughts within!
sentences.inferno-16-s31: He said to me: “Soon from above will come what I am waiting for and what your thought imagines; soon it must reveal itself to your sight.”
sentences.inferno-16-s32: One should always close one’s lips, as long as one can, to that truth which has the face of a lie, because without fault it brings shame; but here I cannot keep silent; and by the verses of this Comedy, reader, I swear to you—may they not be void of lasting grace—that I saw, through that thick and dark air, a figure come swimming upward, astonishing to every steadfast heart, just as a man returns who sometimes goes down to loosen an anchor that has caught on a rock or on something else hidden in the sea, stretching upward and drawing himself in at the feet.
sentences.inferno-16-s25: As that river which first has its own course from Monte Viso toward the east, from the left slope of the Apennines, which is called Acquacheta up above before it plunges down into its lower bed and at Forlì is deprived of that name, roars there above San Benedetto dell’Alpe as it falls in a single descent where there ought to be shelter for a thousand; so, down from a steep bank, we found that dark water resounding, so that in a short time it would have hurt the ear.
sentences.inferno-16-s26: I had a cord girded around me, and with it I had once thought to capture the leopard with the painted skin.
sentences.inferno-16-s27: After I had wholly loosened it from myself, just as the guide had commanded me, I handed it to him knotted and coiled.
sentences.inferno-16-s28: Whereupon he turned toward the right side, and, standing somewhat away from the edge, threw it down into that deep ravine.
